title: Kiely Hine | GIS Portfolio
text:
This is a Quarto website.
To learn more about Quarto websites visit https://quarto.org/docs/websites.

title: Projects
text:
Hydrologic Analysis · GIS Workflow · Climate Data Integration
This project developed a geospatial and statistical workflow to examine how flood timing and seasonal behavior have shifted across Mid-Atlantic watersheds over multiple decades. The work combined long-term streamflow records, climate data, and spatial analysis to identify evolving hydrologic patterns relevant to infrastructure planning and environmental decision-making.






NoteProject focus



Designed a reproducible workflow for integrating environmental time-series data, evaluating seasonal flood behavior, and communicating spatial patterns through GIS-based outputs.








Study area showing stream gage locations across major Mid-Atlantic river basins.


The analysis focused on a regional network of watersheds spanning major Mid-Atlantic river basins. This broader study extent allowed patterns in flood timing to be compared across diverse physiographic and hydrologic settings.






Workflow illustrating the data integration and analysis pipeline.


The project was structured as a multi-stage workflow that linked streamflow records, climate variables, and watershed context into a unified analytical pipeline. The workflow emphasized reproducibility, consistency across sites, and spatial comparability.
Key components included:

geospatial data integration for streamflow and climate inputs

event-based hydrologic analysis for identifying high-flow periods

circular and seasonal analysis for representing cyclical timing behavior

trend evaluation to assess long-term hydrologic change







Spatial variation in flood timing patterns across monitoring sites.


The results indicated a shift toward later and more variable flood timing across parts of the region, along with increased evidence of multimodal seasonal behavior. Patterns also suggested a stronger influence from warm-season precipitation in some watershed systems, pointing to changing flood-generating processes over time.





GIS and data tools
ArcGIS Pro
R
Environmental data pipelines
Spatial data integration

Analytical focus
Spatial-temporal analysis
Hydrologic event analysis
Statistical modeling
Climate-hydrology interpretation





This work demonstrates how GIS can support more than visualization alone. By integrating spatial data management, temporal analysis, and environmental interpretation, the project shows how geospatial workflows can help reveal subtle changes in watershed behavior that matter for planning, risk assessment, and water resource management.
